title: Is big pharma corrupt with John Abramson

chunks.title: Personal Journey with Animal-Based Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker shares his transformative experience after switching to an animal-based diet and using specific supplements. He highlights significant improvements in strength, health, and a range of ailments he faced while on a previous plant-based diet.

chunks.title: The Pharmaceutical Industry and Corruption
chunks.summary: John Abramson discusses his experiences and observations regarding the influence of pharmaceutical companies on medical journals and physician practices. He emphasizes the need for awareness about how data is manipulated to favor corporate profits over patient health.
chunks.transcript: all right John Abramson thank you for coming on the podcast it's a pleasure to have you here well thanks for having me you know I really appreciate your work you've written two books overdosed America and sickening we're going to talk about your work in both of these but also your work involving biox and some of these other drug litigation trials one of the things that's been interesting for me recently as I've sort of stepped away from the day-to-day practice of medicine and do more of this sort of thing the podcasting and the education is trying to help people understand the way that the pharmaceutical industry is really positioned within the medical sphere and I think you are the person that's really done the best job of that so I'm excited to have this conversation today and and and I think it'll be very eye-opening for people so why don't we start with a little bit about your background and then we'll get into I want to talk about vioxx and bextra and Neurontin and then how it all got that way and how we fix it so how did you get to this position well um I was a family doc for 20 years um practiced in a small town an hour north of Boston before I went into practice I did a two-year Robert Johnson Fellowship in research epidemiology and statistics and I thought I was going into a career in academic medicine except in teaching centers family doctors don't get treated all that well and I realized I just want to go out in

chunks.title: Case Study: Vioxx Scandal
chunks.summary: Abramson details the Vioxx scandal, emphasizing how the drug was marketed as safer than alternatives, while the truth about its dangers was concealed. He discusses the broader implications for patient safety and trust in medical recommendations.
chunks.transcript: the community and practice medicine so I did and I entered practice in 1982 and thought I would do that for the rest of my life I loved being a family doc and making house calls and knowing families and little kids and dying older folks and trying to make people's lives better but as the 90s wore on it was very clear to me Paul that the medical journals were becoming increasingly influenced by the drug companies and um things really started to heat up there was an article in the Journal of American Medical Association that showed that hormone replacement therapy significantly increased the risk of breast cancer in post-menopausal women and yet the docs that I worked with couldn't be convinced that they should stop putting healthy post-menopausal women on hormone replacement therapy and then the the straw that broke the camel's back was vioxx um the New England it was a arthritis drug an arthritis and pain drug an anti-inflammatory drug like Motrin or Naprosyn no more effective than than those over-the-counter remedies for pain supposedly a better drug because it decreased the risk of serious GI complications and um an article was published in the New England Journal in November of 2000 that supported that claim research by Merck and three months later the FDA had an advisory committee meeting and there was a link on the web to the data that Merck had sent them and at this point I was getting pretty interested in what was going wrong and I followed that link Upstream into the FDA and found out that Not only was vioxx not a better drug but it was a significantly worse drug so that one out of 40 people who took vioxx had a serious adverse event a serious adverse event is like serious enough to put you in the hospital or worse and that wasn't in the New England Journal article the significant increase in the risk of heart attacks and heart attacks strokes and blood clots was not in the New England Journal uh medicine article and at that point I realized that this thing had gone off the rails the fox was the most heavily advertised drug on TV my patients were coming in demanding it not asking for it demanding it and I realized that we hard-working docs could no longer practice good medicine based on the information that we were getting and was supposed to trust we had been taught to trust and at that point I left my practice to write overdosed America it was published in September of 2004 and I was uh just incredibly lucky that a week after my book came out in 2004 viax was pulled from the market so I was catapulted onto the media scene and on The Today Show and you know the morning shows and all sorts of shows and in the what's now called Legacy Media um great coverage and the lawyers saw me uh on TV speaking English translating science into English for uh regular people non-professionals and started to ask me to be an expert in litigation so I spent uh 10 years after that um serving as an expert in litigation and as an expert you get access to the computers to the to the corporate computers you know everything that everyone always says I wish I really knew what was going on the expert's job is to find out what's going on and if you want data you ask for data and when I saw something wrong with the vioxx data I asked for the data and it was wrong so I spent 10 years in litigation and found a whole lot of things wrong but it wasn't changing the way medicine was practicing docs were still um being um uh influenced heavily influenced by the drug companies because they were publishing the articles in the journals and they were sending their drug reps around with reprints and they were hiring the uh prestigious docs to come out and tell tell uh the practicing docs that these drugs are great and you should prescribe them showing the slides that Merck had prepared not slides they had prepared um and um so after 10 years in litigation which is um it's a Hardball sport I mean it's a blood sport you've got to be really ready to take on teams of lawyers in depositions in the courtroom um and uh after 10 years I had learned a whole lot about how the pharmaceutical industry works but I had not had any impact whatsoever on how doctors think they should get the information they need to prescribe to choose which drugs to prescribe for the patients so at that point again I left what I was doing to write another book it took me six years

chunks.title: Neurontin and Marketing Malpractices
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the drug Neurontin, highlighting how its marketing was fraught with false claims and manipulation of data to suggest efficacy in treating conditions it was never approved for. Abramson describes the drug's ongoing popularity despite questionable efficacy.

chunks.title: Critique of Medical Education
chunks.summary: The discussion underscores the limitations of medical education, particularly regarding the emphasis on pharmaceuticals over lifestyle changes. Abramson argues for a better integration of lifestyle medicine into medical training to prevent chronic diseases.

chunks.title: Government and Industry Collusion
chunks.summary: They analyze the relationship between the FDA and pharmaceutical companies, discussing how conflicts of interest compromise drug approval processes. Abramson argues that the influence of pharma on regulatory bodies leads to dangerous marketing practices.
